old_claim.note: Instrument Calibration Standards provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct Technical Drawing Standards to Instrument Calibration Standards edge remains because the source-backed node is scoped to machine illustration conventions, not calibrated measurement regimes.

source_shape.source_locator: Introduction describes Ramelli's 1588 machine book as 195 engravings and detailed machine descriptions influenced by mathematics and geometry.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source anchors the node in visual engineering communication through engravings and descriptions, without making instrument calibration standards a prerequisite.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Geometric drawing and illustrated machine description can precede formal calibration regimes; the cited scope needs visual/projection practice, not instrument-standard infrastructure.

ontology_before: Technical Drawing Standards depended directly on instrument calibration standards, overconstraining a broad visual communication practice with a measurement-standard dependency.
ontology_after: Technical Drawing Standards retain only perspective/artistic projection context; calibration standards remain relevant to narrower measurement and instrumentation nodes.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: removed the direct instrument_calibration_standards prerequisite. Preserved: technical drawings still derive from visual projection conventions.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to calibrated industrial drafting standards rather than Renaissance machine illustration conventions.
A source shows Ramelli-style machine drawings required formal instrument calibration standards.
A separate precision_engineering_drawings node is introduced and intentionally depends on calibration standards.
short_reason: Calibration standards are later/narrower measurement infrastructure, not a prerequisite for Renaissance technical machine drawings.
